content.text: Translating a lo-fi prototype into production code without information loss requires a disciplined handoff process. When the subject matter is an agentic AI system — where agent decisions, tool calls, and state transitions live alongside conventional UI flows — the handoff becomes more complex than for a standard CRUD application. Every screen annotation must capture not just layout intent but also which agent behavior drives a state change, what the fallback looks like when the model refuses or fails, and how each interaction maps to an acceptance criterion an engineer can verify.
What It Is

Prototype-to-build handoff is the structured transfer of design intent from a lo-fi prototype to the engineering team, together with a traceability system that keeps design decisions, implementation tickets, and test cases linked throughout development. In traditional software the core deliverables are annotated screens, a component inventory, and a design token map. For agentic AI products, the handoff package must additionally specify agent decision points, the complete set of agent-visible context, tool call signatures and expected outputs, and the error-state UI for every way the model can fail.

Traceability is the property that lets any artifact — a Figma screen, an engineering ticket, a test case — be traced forward and backward to every related artifact. A prototype frame has a trace ID. The behavior spec referencing that frame carries the same ID. The engineering ticket implementing that behavior references the spec ID. The automated test validating the ticket quotes the ticket ID. When a designer changes the prototype after sign-off, the trace graph immediately reveals which tickets and tests are now stale.
Why It Matters

Without a structured handoff, design intent erodes predictably. Engineers interpret ambiguous annotations in ways that are technically correct but behaviourally wrong. Edge cases that were obvious in the Figma walkthrough are never communicated to whoever writes the acceptance criteria, so they are never tested. Late-stage design changes trigger undocumented rework as the team discovers, one ticket at a time, which parts of the codebase were downstream of the changed frame.

For agentic AI specifically, the stakes are higher because agent failures often look like application behaviour rather than bugs. If the handoff does not explicitly document what the interface must show when the model returns an unexpected tool call, the engineer fills the gap with a generic error message that provides no recovery path for the user. That silent degradation is invisible to stakeholders until production. A handoff process that makes agent behaviour specs a first-class deliverable prevents this class of defect at source.
Benefits
Reduced rework:
Ambiguity is resolved before code is written, not after a sprint review surfaces a mismatch.
Complete acceptance criteria:
Tickets carry enough specification for QA to write tests without re-interviewing the designer.
Bidirectional traceability:
A late design change propagates automatically to the list of affected tickets and tests rather than being discovered piecemeal.
Agent edge case coverage:
Error states, refusals, and partial outputs are documented alongside happy-path flows, so they are testable.
Onboarding speed:
New engineers can understand why a UI component exists by following the trace link back to the prototype frame and its annotation.
Trade-offs

A rigorous handoff process has real costs. Annotating every agent state, assigning trace IDs, and maintaining the traceability register adds two to four days to the design phase on a typical feature. Teams moving at startup pace sometimes treat this overhead as wasteful, particularly when the prototype was built in a week and will likely change. The honest trade-off is that the handoff tax is paid once per feature, while the rework cost of a poor handoff is paid repeatedly across the sprint, in QA, and again in production bugs.

A second limitation is maintenance burden. If the traceability register is a spreadsheet, it goes stale as soon as no one owns it. Automation through a project management integration (Jira, Linear, GitHub Issues) can transfer some of that maintenance cost onto tooling, but integration setup requires upfront investment. Small teams may find the lightweight version — trace IDs in Figma annotation comments and ticket descriptions — sufficient without a dedicated register.
How It Works

The process runs in four stages. First, the designer completes the lo-fi prototype with all agent states annotated: the default state, the loading/thinking state, each category of agent output (structured response, tool call result, refusal, partial failure), and any state-machine transitions between them. Each annotated frame receives a unique prototype ID following a naming convention such as
PROTO-{feature}-{screen}-{variant}

Second, the behavior specification document is authored alongside the prototype. For agentic features this spec lists every agent decision point on each screen: the prompt structure that triggers a given agent action, the model's expected output schema, the UI mapping for each output variant, and the recovery flow for each failure mode. Each behavior spec entry cross-references at least one prototype ID.

Third, the handoff package is assembled: the annotated prototype export, the behavior spec, an asset inventory (tokens, icons, copy strings), and a pre-created set of engineering tickets whose acceptance criteria are pulled directly from the behavior spec. Ticket IDs are written back into the behavior spec entries they implement, completing the forward link. Fourth, the engineering team conducts an intake review, raising clarification questions which are answered in writing and appended to the relevant spec entries.
Architecture and Flow
The diagram below shows the four-phase handoff flow with the traceability layer running beneath all phases.

attrs.alt: Prototype-to-Build Handoff Flow diagram showing four phases: Lo-Fi Prototype, Design Review, Handoff Package, and Engineering Intake, with a traceability layer connecting all phases bidirectionally

content.text: The traceability layer is the critical addition beyond a standard design-to-dev workflow. Every artifact carries an ID, and the registry records forward (design → build) and backward (build → design) links. When a row in the registry is marked stale — because the prototype frame it references was revised — the cascading impact is immediately visible to anyone querying the registry for tickets or tests with that prototype ID.
When to Use It

A full handoff process with formal traceability is appropriate when: the feature involves agent behavior that engineering has not built before; the design phase included significant stakeholder review that resulted in documented decisions; the team size is large enough that the designer and the engineers implementing the feature are different people; or the feature is regulated, safety-adjacent, or involves financial or medical data.

A lighter-weight version — trace ID comments in Figma and ticket descriptions, without a formal register — is sufficient for: solo or pair implementations where the designer and engineer are the same person; internal tooling with no external users; prototypes built purely for proof-of-concept where the resulting code is intentionally throwaway. The lightweight version retains the most important property — bidirectional linking — while eliminating the maintenance overhead of a separate registry.
How to Implement
Step 1 — Annotate Agent States

In Figma (or your prototype tool), create a dedicated component variant for every agent state per screen. Add annotation notes using a consistent template. A minimal template covers: the agent action that triggers this state, the expected model output type, the UI behaviour in this state, and the fallback or error state if the agent call fails.
Step 2 — Author the Behavior Specification

The behavior spec is a structured document, not a narrative walkthrough. Each entry represents one agent decision point and follows a fixed schema. Below is a JSON representation of one spec entry; teams often use a spreadsheet or a Notion database with the same columns.
Step 3 — Build the Traceability Register
A traceability register is a queryable record of links between artifact types. The minimal schema has five columns: proto_id, spec_id, ticket_id, test_case_id, and status. Status values are:
active
stale
(prototype changed but downstream not updated), and
deprecated
(removed from scope).
Step 4 — Engineering Intake Checklist

The engineering intake review is a synchronous or async session in which the team reads through the handoff package and surfaces open questions. Run the intake against a standard checklist to ensure completeness before the sprint starts.
Every screen in the prototype has at least one behavior spec entry.
Every agent decision point has a documented failure state and a UI recovery path.
Every behavior spec entry has at least one corresponding engineering ticket.
Tickets have acceptance criteria that are testable without re-reading the prototype.
All trace IDs are present in both the spec and the ticket.
Open questions from the intake session are documented in writing, not resolved verbally only.
Examples
Simple: Minimal Handoff for a Single Agent Interaction
Production: Automated Stale-Link Reporting in CI
Real-World Example

A mid-sized enterprise deployed an AI-assisted document review product. The design team produced forty-three prototype frames covering six distinct agent states per document (loading, summarising, classifying, flagging, awaiting human review, completed). Without a traceability system, engineers built twelve of the sixteen agent-error states based on verbal descriptions from a design walkthrough. Four states were never implemented because they were only visible in secondary frames the team skipped during the walkthrough.

After adopting structured handoff with a traceability register in Notion, the same team processed the next feature — a multi-step agent workflow with twenty-eight screens — with zero undocumented states discovered in QA. The behavior spec entries were imported into Jira using a Python script that parsed the spec JSON and created tickets with acceptance criteria pre-populated. The traceability register caught three stale links when the design team iterated on the confirmation modal two weeks after initial sign-off, flagging exactly four tickets for scope update rather than requiring a full re-walkthrough.
Common Mistakes
Annotating only the happy path.

Agent error states are as important as success states; skipping them means engineers have no specification for failure UI. Every agent interaction has at least three states: loading, success, and failure.
Trace IDs only in the register, not in the artifacts.
If IDs are only in the spreadsheet, no one uses them during development. IDs must appear directly in Figma annotations, ticket descriptions, and test case names.
Behavior spec authored after ticket creation.
If tickets are created from memory of the walkthrough, acceptance criteria are incomplete. The spec must be written before tickets, not retroactively.
Verbal answers to intake questions.
Questions raised in the intake session that are answered verbally are lost within days. All answers must be appended in writing to the relevant spec entry.
Treating handoff as a one-time event.
Design changes post-sign-off happen. The process must include a change control step that marks affected trace entries stale and triggers a targeted re-review.
Best Practices
Assign trace IDs as early as the first prototype review, not at handoff time.
Keep the behavior spec in the same system as engineering tickets so links are clickable, not just referenced as strings.
Run the intake as a structured review against the checklist, not an open-ended walkthrough. Time-box it to 90 minutes.
Write acceptance criteria in the Given/When/Then format so they are immediately usable for automated tests.
Include latency targets and model output constraints in the behavior spec, not just visual requirements.
Automate stale-link detection by running a CI check on every design file commit.
Version the behavior spec alongside the prototype so the history of design decisions is preserved.
Performance and Scalability

The handoff process scales linearly with feature complexity. A feature with ten agent decision points requires ten spec entries and produces approximately thirty to fifty tickets, which is manageable manually. At one hundred agent decision points the register becomes difficult to navigate without tooling. At this scale, a database-backed registry with a query API is worth implementing.

The most expensive operation in a large register is stale-link propagation: when a single prototype frame is revised, all downstream entries must be re-validated. The propagation cost is bounded by the fan-out degree of the changed frame — frames referenced by many spec entries are high-risk change points. Designers should prefer creating new frames to revising heavily-referenced existing ones, reducing fan-out.
Tooling and Ecosystem
Figma:
Supports annotation notes and variables for proto IDs; the REST API can export frame metadata for CI integration.
Notion / Confluence:
Databases with relational links between spec entries and tickets work well as lightweight registers.
Jira / Linear:
Custom fields for proto_id and spec_id on ticket types enable automated register queries.
Zephyr / Xray:
Jira-native test case management tools that support linking test cases to requirements — the test-case side of the trace register.
GitHub Issues + Projects:
Open-source teams can store the register as a JSON file in the repo and run the CI stale-check script on every PR.
Comparison with Alternatives
Approach
Traceability
Overhead
Best for
Full handoff with register
Bidirectional, automated
High (3–5 days)
Regulated, complex, large teams
Lightweight (IDs in comments)
Manual, forward only
Low (0.5 days)
Small teams, internal tools
Walkthrough only
None
Minimal
Prototypes, throwaway code
Design system + Storybook
Component-level only
Medium (tool setup)
UI-heavy, non-agent products
Security and Reliability

The handoff package itself can be a security asset: by explicitly documenting which agent actions are permissible from each UI state, the behavior spec creates a functional specification for the authorisation layer. If a screen's spec says the agent can only call
read_document
and not
delete_document
, engineers know the backend must enforce that constraint — and the acceptance criterion makes it testable.

Sensitive information in the behavior spec — prompt templates that reveal safety filtering logic, tool schemas that expose internal API structure — should be treated as internal documentation with the same access controls as the codebase. The spec should not be stored in a public Figma file or a shared Notion page with guest access enabled.
Mental Model

Think of the traceability register as a directed graph where nodes are artifacts (prototype frames, spec entries, tickets, test cases) and edges are 'implements' or 'is-implemented-by' links. The prototype is the root. Every other artifact is a descendant. When a root node changes, the standard graph algorithm for finding all descendants gives exactly the blast radius of the change. The register is just a materialised view of that graph, optimised for human querying rather than traversal.

This mental model also explains why IDs must be in the artifacts themselves: in a graph, edges are properties of nodes, not of an external spreadsheet. If a ticket does not contain its prototype reference, the graph is disconnected at that node — traceability breaks silently.
Key Takeaways
A structured prototype-to-build handoff reduces rework by resolving design ambiguity before code is written.
For agentic AI products, the behavior spec must cover agent decision points, tool call schemas, and all UI states including failure modes — not just the visual layout.
Trace IDs must appear inside the artifacts themselves (Figma annotations, ticket descriptions, test case names) — a register that is separate from the artifacts provides no practical traceability.
Stale-link detection should be automated in CI so design changes post-sign-off trigger a targeted review of affected tickets rather than a full walkthrough.
The intake checklist — not the walkthrough — is the quality gate: if a spec entry cannot pass the checklist, it is not ready for development.
Acceptance criteria should be in Given/When/Then format so they map directly to automated tests without re-interpretation.
Scale the process to the team: a full register for regulated or complex features; trace IDs in comments for small internal tools; skip only for throwaway prototypes.
